result.markdown:
--- title: "Bringing Work Trips Closer to Home" subtitle: "Wasatch Front Regional Travel Demand Model — Sketch-Level HBW Trip Redistribution Test" format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 toc-title: "Contents" embed-resources: true theme: flatly execute: echo: false warning: false message: false jupyter: july2025 --- **[← All run sets](../../index.qmd)**  |  [Slides view](slides.html) *Prepared by Bill Hereth · last updated 2026-07-11* ::: {.callout-note} ## Data completeness as of this rendering **9** of **9** planned shift scenarios have a verified run: Closer01, Closer02, Closer03, Closer04, Closer05, Closer06, Closer07, Closer08, Closer09 — covering City Area, Medium District, and Workshop Area at the **5%, 10%, 25%** shift level(s). Most charts below have buttons (top-right of the chart) to toggle between shift levels; a few tables and narrative figures stay fixed at **10%** (noted where that applies). Extends automatically as more scenarios complete — see this run set's `REPORT_PLAN.md`. ::: ## The Question What would it look like, in rough terms, if more people who live in an area of our region also worked in that area — instead of commuting elsewhere? This sketch-level test asks: if a share of home-based-work (HBW) trips that currently start inside a target area but end outside it instead ended inside the target area — following today's existing internal destination pattern — what would change at the corridor and regional level? Three geography types are tested, each applied region-wide (every area of that type shifted simultaneously): **City Area** (roughly a city boundary, ~55 areas), **Medium District** (~35 areas), and **Workshop Area** (~8 multi-city groupings). Per the approved scoping memo, **City Area is the lead geography for this write-up**; Medium District and Workshop Area are shown throughout as a robustness check across scales. The model basis is the **2027 RTP adoption year** network and land use, model version **v10.0.0-beta.1**. This is not a land-use forecast. It is a directional, order-of-magnitude check on whether "closer jobs-housing balance" is worth studying further — see [Caveats](#caveats-and-interpretation). --- ## Plain-Language Takeaways - At a **10% shift**, **City Area** redistribution reduces region-wide Peak-period vehicle-hours of delay (VHD) by roughly **27,887 hours/day** and Peak-period VMT by roughly **1,160,782 miles/day** — a small fraction of regional totals, but directionally consistent with what the mechanism should produce. - Peak-period vehicle-hours of travel per household falls by about **3.2 minutes/day** region-wide under the City Area scenario — the closest read on "time given back to households." - Medium District and Workshop Area shifts produce **similar-direction, somewhat smaller** reductions at this shift level — see the [cross-scale comparison](#cross-scale-comparison). - **Transit ridership on TRAX, FrontRunner, and bus all decline slightly** under all three geography types — shortening commutes reduces the number of trips long enough for transit to compete on, an important, non-obvious side effect (see [Transit Ridership](#transit-ridership)). - Most charts below let you **toggle between shift levels**, and the VMT/VHD/VHT charts also toggle between **Peak and Off-Peak** periods, as more scenarios complete — see [Caveats](#caveats-and-interpretation) before drawing conclusions regardless of level. --- ## How the Test Works {#how-the-test-works} Home-based-work trips starting inside a target area but ending outside it are identified in the model's trip table. A share of those trips (5%, 10%, or 25% — 25% not yet run) is proportionally redistributed to end inside the target area instead, following the *existing* internal destination pattern (a location that already attracts 15% of work trips into the area gets 15% of the newly shifted trips too). This redistribution is applied to the HBW destination-choice matrix **before mode choice**; everything downstream (mode choice, assignment) runs normally on the adjusted trip pattern. Everything else about the region — total trips, population, other destinations — is unchanged. ::: {#tbl-scenarios .cell execution_count=5} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Scenarios with a verified run as of this rendering
Scenario Geography Type Shift Level (%)
Closer00 Baseline 0
Closer04 City Area 5
Closer05 Medium District 5
Closer06 Workshop Area 5
Closer01 City Area 10
Closer02 Medium District 10
Closer03 Workshop Area 10
Closer07 City Area 25
Closer08 Medium District 25
Closer09 Workshop Area 25
``` ::: ::: --- ## Congestion: Vehicle-Hours of Delay {#congestion-vehicle-hours-of-delay} This is the headline metric — congestion is what people actually feel, more than raw traffic volume. Vehicle-hours of delay (VHD) is summed across all facility types and shown by county and region, for all three geography types. Use the buttons to switch shift levels. ::: {#cell-fig-vhd-county .cell execution_count=6} ::: {#fig-vhd-county .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Salt Lake and Utah counties see the largest absolute VHD reductions under every geography type, consistent with where the region's congestion (and therefore its redistribution opportunity) is concentrated. **City Area** produces the largest region-wide VHD reduction of the three geography types, though the gap to Medium District is small. ### By Facility Type ::: {#cell-fig-vhd-facility .cell execution_count=7} ::: {#fig-vhd-facility .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Freeways carry most of the region's delay reduction in absolute terms — expected, since that's where most of the region's VHD sits today. Arterial and collector reductions are smaller but consistent in direction. --- ## Why VHD Moved: Vehicle Miles Traveled {#why-vhd-moved-vehicle-miles-traveled} VMT is explanatory here, not the headline: shorter commutes mean less driving, and less driving on congested facilities is why delay falls. ::: {#cell-fig-vmt-county .cell execution_count=8} ::: {#fig-vmt-county .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Region-wide, the shift reduces daily VMT across all geography types — City Area and Medium District produce similar-sized reductions; Workshop Area's is somewhat smaller, consistent with it being the coarsest geography (fewer, larger areas mean some "shifted" trips were already relatively close by). --- ## Corridor Detail {#corridor-detail} Volume and delay change on the region's major corridors, City Area first (the lead geography), **fixed at the 10% shift level, Peak period** (this table isn't a Plotly chart, so the toggle buttons above don't apply to it). Corridors are ranked by City Area's VHD impact. ::: {#tbl-corridor-city .cell execution_count=9} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
City Area (10% shift) — corridor volume and delay change vs. baseline
Corridor Daily Volume Δ Volume Daily VHD (hrs) Δ VHD (hrs)
I-15 6,603,020 -389,108 32,693 -13,893
Redwood Road 967,967 -60,318 6,834 -1,899
Mountain View Corridor 701,282 -28,646 5,204 -1,468
I-215 892,306 -65,223 1,780 -1,073
Bangerter Highway 555,234 -24,222 3,966 -996
State Street 1,171,719 -68,821 2,069 -656
Legacy Parkway 126,394 -12,067 456 -528
US-89 (north Davis) 707,864 -51,820 881 -307
SR-73 114,709 -5,938 797 -230
SR-201 239,884 -6,369 955 -176
I-80 606,170 -15,070 817 -124
9000 South 360,396 -7,480 902 -93
3300 South 339,944 -8,810 419 -63
12300 South 111,514 -4,236 346 -54
University Pkwy 172,457 -3,775 504 -41
Porter Rockwell 39,211 -1,674 187 -36
5600 South 103,446 -2,004 175 -19
2100 N Lehi 133,008 -6,999 287 -11
Antelope Dr 76,250 -992 154 -10
West Davis Corridor 15,645 -2,306 1 -0
``` ::: ::: I-15 accounts for the largest single share of City Area's regional VHD reduction, followed by Redwood Road, Mountain View Corridor, I-215, and Bangerter Highway — the region's highest-volume, most delay-prone facilities. ### Same Corridors, Medium District and Workshop Area ::: {#cell-fig-corridor-compare .cell execution_count=10} ::: {#fig-corridor-compare .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: The same corridors move in the same direction under all three geography types, at broadly similar (though not identical) magnitudes — a useful robustness check on the City Area result above. ### Freeway Corridors by County {#freeway-by-county} Freeway/expressway corridors carry the bulk of the region's through traffic; a shared-name corridor like I-15 actually spans several counties, so here each freeway corridor is broken into the county each segment sits in instead of one blended region-wide total. City Area only (the lead geography), toggle by shift level and Peak/Off-Peak. ::: {#cell-fig-freeway-county .cell execution_count=11} ::: {#fig-freeway-county .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: I-15's reduction is concentrated in Salt Lake and Utah counties, where its own daily VHD is largest to begin with; corridors that only pass through one or two counties (Legacy Parkway, SR-201) naturally show a simpler single- or two-county picture. ### Corridor Orientation: North/South vs. East/West {#corridor-orientation} Every named corridor above is also grouped by its predominant real-world orientation — I-215 loops around Salt Lake County and doesn't fit either category cleanly, so it gets its own "Loop" bucket rather than being forced into one. ::: {#cell-fig-orientation .cell execution_count=12} ::: {#fig-orientation .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: North/south corridors (I-15, Redwood Road, State Street, Legacy Parkway, Mountain View Corridor, Bangerter Highway, US-89) carry the large majority of the named-corridor VHD reduction — expected, since the region's heaviest-volume freeways and arterials run north/south along the valley. East/west corridors and the I-215 loop see smaller, but still consistently negative, changes. --- ## Transit Ridership {#transit-ridership} Boardings for FrontRunner, each TRAX line, and a "Bus" category aggregating every non-rail route (local, BRT, express, and premium service combined). ::: {#cell-fig-transit .cell execution_count=13} ::: {#fig-transit .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Boardings decline slightly across every rail line and the bus aggregate, under every geography type. This is a real, non-obvious mechanical effect: bringing work trips closer to home shortens the commutes most likely to have used transit in the first place, so some riders shift to shorter drives, walks, or bikes instead. FrontRunner sees the largest absolute rail decline, consistent with it serving the longest-distance commutes; the bus aggregate's decline reflects the same effect summed across roughly 160 local/BRT/express routes. --- ## Free Time Added Back: VHT per Household {#free-time-added-back} This metric deliberately uses **vehicle-hours of travel (VHT)**, not VHD. VHD (used in the [congestion section](#congestion-vehicle-hours-of-delay) above) only captures the delay component of travel time; VHT is the full realized travel time — free-flow time plus delay — so it also picks up the time households save simply because their trips are now *shorter*, not only because those trips hit less congestion. That's the more complete read on "time given back to households." ::: {#cell-fig-vht-hh .cell execution_count=14} ::: {#fig-vht-hh .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Region-wide, VHT per household falls under every geography type at both Peak and Off-Peak — a small but directionally meaningful amount of time given back to the average household, on top of whatever congestion relief accrues to specific corridors above. --- ## HBW Trip Length {#hbw-trip-length} ::: {#cell-fig-trip-length .cell execution_count=15} ::: {#fig-trip-length .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Average HBW trip length falls region-wide under every geography type — the most direct measure of the mechanism actually working as intended: trips that used to travel farther for work now travel a shorter distance, on average, across the whole region. ### Trip Length Distribution {#trip-length-distribution} The average above is one number; the full distribution shows *how* the shift happens — fewer long-distance commutes, more short ones — built directly from the origin-destination trip matrix and distance skim (region-wide, all HBW trips regardless of mode, in 2-mile bins), not the production-side estimate used for the average. The baseline curve is shown alongside whichever shift level is selected. ::: {#cell-fig-trip-length-dist .cell execution_count=16} ::: {#fig-trip-length-dist .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Cross-check against the production-side average above, **fixed at the 10% shift level** (independent methodology — full O-D skim × trip volume, not PMT/trips): - **City Area**: weighted-average trip length changes by **-0.96 miles**. - **Medium District**: weighted-average trip length changes by **-0.94 miles**. - **Workshop Area**: weighted-average trip length changes by **-0.74 miles**. --- ## What This Means for Specific Cities {#city-level-results} The City Area scenario redistributes every city's own outbound HBW trips simultaneously — here's what that looks like for five specific cities picked to span the region's size and geography: **Ogden, Layton, Salt Lake City, West Jordan, and Saratoga Springs**. This section is only meaningful under the City Area geography type, since that's the one whose redistribution boundaries are these same cities; Medium District and Workshop Area use different (larger, differently-shaped) target areas and aren't shown here. ::: {#cell-fig-city-trip-length .cell execution_count=18} ::: {#fig-city-trip-length .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {#cell-fig-city-vht .cell execution_count=19} ::: {#fig-city-vht .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {#tbl-city .cell execution_count=20} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
City Area, 10% shift — production-side change by city (this table is fixed at 10%, not toggle-enabled)
City Δ Daily HBW VMT Δ Peak VHT/HH (min) Δ Trip Length (mi)
Saratoga Springs -98,780 -6.41 -1.77
Layton -47,282 -1.67 -0.97
West Jordan -58,802 -2.14 -0.86
Ogden -39,712 -1.43 -0.78
Salt Lake City -53,912 -0.87 -0.33
``` ::: ::: Saratoga Springs sees the largest trip-length and time reduction of the five, plausibly because it's the newest/most rapidly-growing of the five with the most outbound commuting to begin with, so a proportional shift back in has more room to matter. Salt Lake City sees the smallest change — unsurprising, since it already has the region's largest, most diverse local job base, so a smaller share of its residents' HBW trips end up leaving the city in the first place. --- ## Mode Share {#mode-share} ::: {.callout-note} Supplemental context, not a memo-mandated headline metric. ::: ::: {#cell-fig-mode-share .cell execution_count=21} ::: {#fig-mode-share .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: HBW mode share barely moves at any shift level — consistent with the mechanism only touching trip *destinations*, not the mode choice model's own logic. The small transit decline visible here matches the ridership drop discussed above. --- ## Cross-Scale Comparison {#cross-scale-comparison} All three geography types, side by side, **fixed at the 10% shift level, Peak period** (this table isn't toggle-enabled). Region-wide figures. ::: {#tbl-cross-scale .cell execution_count=22} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Region-wide change vs. baseline, 10% shift level, Peak period
Geography Type Δ VHD (hrs) Δ VMT (mi) Δ Peak VHT/HH (min) Δ HBW Trip Length (mi) Δ Transit Boardings
City Area -27,887 -1,160,782 -3.20 -0.953 -5,209
Medium District -27,587 -1,149,815 -3.16 -0.926 -5,726
Workshop Area -23,716 -962,672 -2.65 -0.719 -4,087
``` ::: ::: At the 10% shift level, City Area and Medium District produce broadly similar regional effects; Workshop Area's are consistently the smallest of the three — plausible given it's the coarsest geography (only 8 areas region-wide, so "outside the area" captures fewer actually-nearby destinations than City Area or Medium District would). --- ## Caveats and Interpretation {#caveats-and-interpretation} This is a **sketch-level, order-of-magnitude test**, not a land-use or economic forecast. Specifically: - **Not a real land-use or economic forecast.** No businesses actually relocate, no zoning changes, no job growth is simulated — the trip table is manually forced to behave as if that happened. - **Feasibility is not checked.** The model does not confirm the target area could physically hold the added jobs (land, buildings, parking). - **It's a sketch, not a precise estimate.** Trips are manually reallocated using today's existing destination pattern rather than letting the model's own distribution logic decide — results are order-of-magnitude, not precise numbers. - **Only home-based-work trips are touched.** Other trip purposes (shopping, school, etc.) are unchanged. - **No second-order effects.** Housing costs, people moving closer to new jobs, or businesses responding to changed traffic patterns are not captured. - **Three shift levels test sensitivity to size, not to method.** 5%, 10%, and 25% (once all are run) all use the same proportional redistribution logic — this is not a test of alternative ways to choose which trips move or where they land. The toggle buttons let you compare magnitudes across levels, not alternative mechanisms. - **Corridor orientation and freeway groupings are a manual simplification.** Each named corridor is assigned one predominant orientation/classification for this sketch-level rollup, not a segment-by-segment geometric analysis — see the Appendix. --- ## Appendix {#appendix} **Mechanism.** Implemented in `run_sets/bring-work-trips-closer-to-home/scripts/redistribute_hbw_trips.py`, wired into the `__HailMary_1Subfolder_closer.s` driver script: the HBW destination-choice matrix is converted to OMX, redistributed using a TAZ-level geography lookup (`CITY_UGRC` / `DISTMED` / `CITYGRP` fields on `WFv1000_TAZ.dbf`), then converted back — applied before mode choice. **Model version and vintage.** `v10.0.0-beta.1-closer-to-home`, 2027 RTP adoption-year network and land use, baseline Control Center `_ControlCenter - OY_2027.block`. **Scenario design.** See [scenario table](#how-the-test-works) above; full config in `run_sets/bring-work-trips-closer-to-home/run_set.yaml` and `scenarios/`. **A note on run status.** Closer01–06's `run_metadata.json` originally recorded these runs as `failed` (Cube Voyager's cluster-shutdown step returned a nonzero exit code after the model had already completed and written all outputs) — verified by inspecting each run's Voyager print log, which shows every step completing and `TOTAL MODEL RUN TIME` logged before the exit-code issue. Outputs were re-curated via `tdmruns import-manual-run`, which recorded a clean `success` status against the same underlying model run. **Shift-level toggle.** Most charts pre-build every available shift level's traces and use Plotly's `updatemenus` (client-side show/hide buttons) to switch between them, since a static Quarto render has no backend to re-run Python on a click. A handful of tables and narrative figures are pandas-styled dataframes, not Plotly figures, so the same toggle doesn't apply to them — those stay fixed at the 10% shift level and say so explicitly. Most of those same charts also carry a second, independent row of buttons ("Absolute" / "% Change") to view each value as a percent of its own baseline instead of a raw unit change — useful for judging a change's size relative to what it's changing from, not just its absolute magnitude. It's independent of the shift-level toggle (it restyles trace values rather than trace visibility), so the two can be used in any combination. **Freeway/orientation groupings.** `run_sets/bring-work-trips-closer-to-home/report_loader.py`'s `FREEWAY_CORRIDORS` and `CORRIDOR_ORIENTATION` are a manual, corridor-level classification (not a per-segment geometric analysis): freeway/expressway status matches the scoping memo's own grouping and is consistent with each corridor's dominant `FTCLASS` in `Summary_SEGID.csv`; orientation (N/S vs. E/W) reflects each corridor's predominant real-world heading, except I-215, which loops around Salt Lake County and gets its own "Loop" category rather than being forced into either. **City-level results.** The five cities (`TARGET_CITIES` in `report_loader.py`) were picked to span the region's size range, from a small, newer, rapidly-growing city (Saratoga Springs) to the region's largest, most job-dense city (Salt Lake City), with a mix of established (Ogden, Layton) and mid-size (West Jordan) cities in between. Matched to `WFv1000_TAZ.dbf`'s `CITY_UGRC` codes (`OGD`/`LAY`/`SLC`/`WJC`/`SAR`); only meaningful under the City Area scenarios, since that's the geography type whose redistribution is actually scoped by these same city boundaries. **Bus aggregate.** `_transit_brding_summary_route.csv`'s own `Mode` field distinguishes TRAX/streetcar rail (7) and FrontRunner commuter rail (8) from everything else (4 = local bus, 5 = BRT, 6 = express, 9 = premium/other) — the "Bus (all non-rail)" category sums every route in that latter group (roughly 160 individual route codes) rather than enumerating them by name. **Data completeness.** See the note at the top of this page for the current count of verified shift scenarios. Corridor definitions: `run_sets/bring-work-trips-closer-to-home/inputs/corridor_segid_crosswalk.csv`, built from the network's link file (`tdm/1_Inputs/3_Highway/GIS/WFv1000_MasterNet_*.dbf`) with manual keyword matching and priority tie-breaking at interchange segments — see `REPORT_PLAN.md` for the full method. **Trip length distribution data.** The distribution above (and its weighted-average cross-check) is built from two curated matrix extracts, not from a summary CSV: the `GP_Dist` table of the final daily highway skim (`5_AssignHwy/5_FinalNetSkims/Skm_DY.mtx`) and the `motor`/`nonmotor` tables of the post-mode-choice HBW trip matrix (`4_ModeChoice/2_DetailedTripMatrices/HBW_trips_allsegs_pkok.mtx`) — both several hundred MB in full, curated down to small single/two-table OMX files via a new `outputs.include` `matrix:`/`tabs:` entry type (see `run_set.yaml`), which invokes Cube Voyager's CONVERTMAT at curation time. The trip matrix's raw values are stored at 100x scale (confirmed empirically against `_ZoneSummary_TripsByMode.csv`'s trusted total, within 0.001%) and divided back down in `report_loader.py`. Distance-skim cells at Cube's `NOACCESS` sentinel (9999, unconnected zone pairs) are excluded before binning, now in 5-mile bins.
